examples.title: Simulating Transaction Confirmation Process
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how transactions flow through blockchain and how consensus affects confirmation time, illustrating scalability limitations.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 FUNCTION broadcast_transaction(transaction):
 ADD transaction TO mempool
 SET status = "pending"
 RETURN status

 FUNCTION wait_for_confirmation(transaction, max_attempts):
 attempts = 0
 WHILE attempts < max_attempts:
 IF transaction IN valid_block:
 RETURN "confirmed"
 ELSE:
 WAIT block_time (Bitcoin: 10 min, Ethereum: 12-14 sec)
 attempts = attempts + 1
 RETURN "timeout - low fee or network congestion"

 // Illustrating scalability limitation
 FUNCTION calculate_throughput(block_size, block_time):
 max_tps = (block_size_bytes / avg_transaction_size) / block_time_seconds
 RETURN max_tps
 // Bitcoin: ~7 TPS, Ethereum: ~15-30 TPS
END
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates why blockchain has limited TPS. The 'wait_for_confirmation' shows why we must wait for block creation, and 'calculate_throughput' directly shows how block size and block time mathematically limit transactions per second.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for finding transaction in blocks

examples.title: Demonstrating 51% Attack Scenario
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how an attacker with majority hash power can manipulate the blockchain, illustrating a key security limitation of blockchain.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 FUNCTION honest_mining(blockchain, honest_power):
 WHILE network_active:
 new_block = mine_block(blockchain.tip, honest_power)
 ADD new_block TO blockchain
 IF blockchain.length > attacker_chain.length:
 SWITCH_TO_longest_chain()

 FUNCTION attack_mining(blockchain, attacker_power):
 secret_chain = CREATE new_chain(blockchain.tip)
 WHILE network_active:
 new_block = mine_block(secret_chain.tip, attacker_power)
 ADD new_block TO secret_chain
 IF secret_chain.length > blockchain.length + 2:
 BROADCAST secret_chain
 // Result: Double-spend attack succeeds

 // KEY INSIGHT: If attacker_hash_rate > 50%, attack succeeds
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the 51% attack mechanism. With majority hash power, an attacker creates a longer secret chain that replaces the honest chain when broadcast - enabling double-spending. This is why network decentralization matters for security.

examples.title: Immutability Verification Process
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how blockchain ensures immutability through cryptographic hashing, making past transactions impossible to alter.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 FUNCTION calculate_block_hash(block):
 header_data = block.version + block.previous_hash + 
 block.merkle_root + block.timestamp + block.difficulty
 RETURN SHA256(header_data)

 FUNCTION verify_chain_integrity(blockchain):
 FOR i FROM 1 TO blockchain.length:
 current_block = blockchain[i]
 computed_hash = calculate_block_hash(current_block)
 IF computed_hash != current_block.hash:
 RETURN FALSE
 IF i > 1 AND current_block.previous_hash != blockchain[i-1].hash:
 RETURN FALSE
 RETURN TRUE

 FUNCTION attempt_modify_block(blockchain, index, new_data):
 blockchain[index].data = new_data
 blockchain[index].hash = calculate_block_hash(blockchain[index])
 FOR i FROM index+1 TO blockchain.length:
 blockchain[i].previous_hash = blockchain[i-1].hash
 blockchain[i].hash = calculate_block_hash(blockchain[i])
 RETURN blockchain
END
examples.explanation: This demonstrates why blockchain is immutable. To modify any past transaction, an attacker must recompute ALL subsequent block hashes faster than the honest network adds new blocks - computationally infeasible for established blockchains.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for verification, O(n²) for modification
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for verification, O(n) for modification
